title: How stress affects the body and mind
citation: McEwen, B.S. (1998). Stress, adaptation, and disease: Allostasis and allostatic load. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 840, 33-44.
content:
When stress is chronic, its effects accumulate across the body and mind in ways that go well beyond feeling tense or overwhelmed. Understanding these effects can help explain why prolonged stress takes such a significant toll on health and functioning.

At a physiological level, chronic stress keeps the body's stress response partially activated over time. Cortisol, the primary stress hormone, is released in elevated quantities. In the short term, cortisol is helpful: it mobilises energy, suppresses inflammation, and sharpens attention. But sustained high levels of cortisol begin to cause harm. The immune system becomes less effective, increasing vulnerability to infection and slowing recovery from illness. Sleep is disrupted, because cortisol naturally opposes the hormones that promote deep rest. Chronic sleep disruption then compounds fatigue, mood difficulties, and cognitive impairment in a reinforcing cycle.

Biologist Bruce McEwen introduced the concept of allostatic load to describe the cumulative wear on the body caused by repeated or sustained stress responses. Allostasis is the process by which the body achieves stability through change, adapting its physiology to meet demands. Allostatic load is the cost of that adaptation over time. High allostatic load has been associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, metabolic problems, and accelerated ageing at the cellular level.

The effects on the mind are equally significant. Chronic stress impairs concentration and working memory, making it harder to learn, plan, or think clearly. It increases emotional reactivity: situations that might otherwise feel manageable can trigger disproportionate frustration, irritability, or upset. Anxiety and depression are more likely to develop in the context of sustained stress, partly because the neurobiological systems involved in stress regulation overlap with those implicated in mood and anxiety disorders.

Recognising the signs of chronic stress, and taking them seriously rather than normalising them, is an important step toward addressing its impact.
